title: Allow podman run against specgen json
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

Now that specgen has been merged into mainline podman, it would be nice to give `podman run` the ability to load a generated spec json from disk, rather than needing to pass dozens of options to it.

Specifically, this would solve some issues that neither the docker-compose shim nor `podman play kube` solve, because it would allow control of podman-specific knobs like `--cgroups=split` and `--sdnotify=conmon`.

comments.body: I'm in favor of this - seems easy to implement and useful.

This would be super useful for us - it would allow an ecosystem of tools to be built which manipulate the JSON, which is much cleaner than cobbling together and then saving a string of complex command line params.

SGTM, a really cool idea

Cc: @baude @rhatdan

How should this work with other options? Should it
a) allow to set other options to overwrite stuff from the given specgen or
b) error if any other options are set since they should be set in the specgen json?

> How should this work with other options? Should it
> a) allow to set other options to overwrite stuff from the given specgen or

That sounds reasonable to me.

The user-specified specgen would be used for "initialization" and options can override.  In a way, that can be an alternative to using a custom containers.conf, so we could use an environment variable to specify that specgen in addition to a CLI flag.

I almost see this as a separate command, with no options beyond the Specgen to use - seems like that's the easiest way to implement. Accepting a base SpecGen in run could be complicated in that we'd have to deal with defaults overwriting it.
podman play specgen ...?

> podman play specgen ...?

There was a plan to have `podman kube {down,play}` etc. so I am no fan of adding more things to `play`. The desired command really belongs under `podman container`.

I imagine rather a `--specgen=path` flag for `podman {create,run}`.  If it's a `SpecGenerator`, the code changes would be minimal.

My problem with it being a part of a current command is that you need to say all other options are not available.
podman run --specgen=path --security-opt seccomp:unconfined -d sleep 1000
podman run --specgen=path --security-opt seccomp:unconfined -d alpine sleep 1000

What do we do here?

How about 
podman container createspec SPEC.FILE
podman container runspec SPEC.FILE

> My problem with it being a part of a current command is that you need to say all other options are not available. 

Can you elaborate? I think they can still be used, see below.

> podman run --specgen=path --security-opt seccomp:unconfined -d sleep 1000 podman run --specgen=path --security-opt seccomp:unconfined -d alpine sleep 1000
> 
> What do we do here?

The specified spec is the baseline.  Other CLI options allow for changing that baseline.

If the spec says "option=foo" and we have `--option=bar`, the container will run with "bar".

But one thing that just came to my mind is how to deal with the `IMAGE [ARGS]`.  Since the specgen could have both specified, it remains largely unclear whether the first unknown argument would refer to an image or a command.

> How about
podman container createspec SPEC.FILE
podman container runspec SPEC.FILE

SGTM :heavy_check_mark:
I like this idea... I can implement this summer if no one else wants to take this?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
working on this...
@coandco as the original reporter, i'm trying to understand why you needed this?  can you elaborate on your use case? more verbose == better so I can understand it more.

My personal use case is to make my systemd units more elegant.  As an example, here's a genericised example of what one of my units looks like currently:
```
[Unit]
Description=Podman servicename
Documentation=man:podman-generate-systemd(1)
After=network-online.target

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
Environment=IMAGE_NAME=local_registry:5050/servicename
Environment=IMAGE_TAG=prod-20220627-1
Restart=on-failure
KillMode=mixed
ExecStartPre=-rm -f %t/%N.cid
ExecStopPost=-/usr/bin/podman rm -f -i --cidfile=%t/%N.cid
ExecStopPost=-rm -f %t/%N.cid
Delegate=yes
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all
SyslogIdentifier=%N
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run \
    --net=host --replace --rm -d --name=systemd-%N \
    --log-driver=journald --cidfile=/%t/%N.cid  \
    --runtime=/usr/bin/crun --cgroups=split --sdnotify=conmon \
    --env-file /env-files/%n.env \
    -v /host/dir:/container/dir \
    ${IMAGE_NAME}:${IMAGE_TAG} \
    /command/to/start.sh

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

If I could replace that entire ExecStart line with something like `podman container runspec /container-specs/%N.spec`, it would drastically simplify the unit file and make it far easier to change how the container is run without having to cobble together a command line and deal with escaping/spacing/etc.

Basically what I'm looking for is a way to bring up a container from a file reference in a way that allows me to specify all of the podman-specific options like `--cgroups` and `--sdnotify` so it'll play nicely with systemd.

The problem with both the Kubernetes-YAML and docker-compose approaches is that by their very nature they can't allow tweaking of podman-specific knobs, since their specs are determined by non-podman projects.  That's why I was excited when podman announced the specfile effort, as the all-encompassing nature of cloning an existing pod meant that the podman-specific options would have to be part of the spec.

I've looked into Quadlet, and several aspects of the above example systemd unit file are based on the best practices found in https://github.com/containers/quadlet/blob/main/docs/ContainerSetup.md, but I'd like to retain the ability to tweak individual aspects of the container setup to my needs.  I believe that having a docker-compose-like ability to fully specify a container from a specfile would neatly solve my problem.
Makes sense to me, I would like to make it a bit more human usable though. YAML rather the json for example.
Well, since JSON is a strict subset of YAML, if you have it parse the file as YAML it'll transparently work with autogenerated JSON specs while still allowing users to make their own YAML specs, yes?

My point being that humans find it easier in general to work with YAML files than JSON files.  If we are going to export the SPEC, I would prefer that the exported file be a YAML file, so that you could easily edit it and create a new container off of it.  As opposed to handling you a JSON file that you need to edit.

Sure.  Either way, going from "no way to specify all of a container's options in a file" to "able to specify all of a container's options in a file" feels like a huge improvement.  "The specfile is in JSON" to "the specfile is in YAML" feels like a modest quality of life improvement.

> I've looked into Quadlet, and several aspects of the above example systemd unit file are based on the best practices found in https://github.com/containers/quadlet/blob/main/docs/ContainerSetup.md, but I'd like to retain the ability to tweak individual aspects of the container setup to my needs. I believe that having a docker-compose-like ability to fully specify a container from a specfile would neatly solve my problem.

I have a "feeling" that extending Quadlet for your needs may be worth a shot.  There are plans to move Quadlet over into this repository (and migrate it to Go), such that Podman and Quadlet can share the generator code.

I like extending Quadlet as much as possible, but eventually it is going to get very complex and we will have to add a get out of jail free card to it to allow the specificaion of addiitonal_podman_options flag to the unit file.

I think we could just add an experimental feature for now to allow the use of the specgen.

BTW I was talking to @mattdm yesterday, and it pointed out that as an administrator he loves the idea of Quadlet, since it allows him to specify what he wants for running a container in a unit file, and then as Podman improves, his service gets the improvement without him needed to change anything.  
IE If we improve the general security of podman or take advantage of new features of the OS, he can get them as default.  Whereas with the current podman generate systemd, he thinks that we hard code some flags of Podman into the unit file.

Specifically he has watched as podman generate systemd has evolved. Just food for thought.
I recall the mail thread :) I chatted with @baude to prioritize migrating Quadlet over to containers/podman. This way, Quadlet and Podman are always in sync and share the generator code.
@vrothberg @rhatdan @baude should we meet to discuss which path we want to go down for this issue? All of these seem like interesting options to me so I'd like to help in the development!
Yes, I am in favor of a dedicated meeting for this topic.

...wouldn't it make sense to implement run-from-spec even if Quadlet is the way forward for systemd units?  It seems like run-from-spec is pretty low-lift, high-reward, since the groundwork for it has already been laid.

Apologies, I didn't mean to close but clicked on the wrong button.

> ...wouldn't it make sense to implement run-from-spec even if Quadlet is the way forward for systemd units? It seems like run-from-spec is pretty low-lift, high-reward, since the groundwork for it has already been laid.

Things have to be maintained and new feature requests will fly in once a command is there while time is limited.  Weighing the cost-benefit ratio is challenging.

I generally prefer keeping things small.  But that does not mean the feature won't happen :)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@cdoern any update on this one?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@rhatdan what should we do about this?
I thought you were working on this?
Didn't we decide that we would table this until `generate spec` merged? Cc/ @baude
Yes, but this is part of that effort.
